title: Reported deaths from rabies
subtitle: Reported annual deaths from [rabies](#dod:rabies) in humans. Depending on the country's reporting system, this may include laboratory-confirmed cases only, or laboratory-confirmed _and_ clinically-diagnosed cases.
note: Reported deaths are limited by surveillance capacity.
caption:
Reported deaths from rabies

Reported annual deaths from rabies in humans. Depending on the country's reporting system, this may include laboratory-confirmed cases only, or laboratory-confirmed and clinically-diagnosed cases.

This choropleth world map displays reported annual deaths from rabies in humans for the year 2022. The legend uses a color scale ranging from light gray for "No reported deaths" to dark brown for values exceeding 300, with intermediate thresholds at 1, 3, 10, 30, and 100. The darkest brown shade, indicating more than 300 reported deaths, is visible in India and China. Surrounding these nations, countries such as Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Indonesia appear in dark orange shades that correspond to the range between 30 and 100 deaths. In Africa, nations including Nigeria, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Ethiopia sit in mid-range orange bands, generally falling between 10 and 30 reported deaths. The United States, Brazil, and Russia are colored in lighter orange shades, placing them in the lower end of the scale, typically between 1 and 10 deaths. A large portion of the map, including most of Europe, Canada, Australia, and the southern cone of South America, is shaded in light gray, signifying no reported deaths for the year. Additionally, distinct diagonal hatching covers Greenland, much of Central Africa, and several territories in the Middle East and Oceania, denoting areas with no data. The visualization highlights a concentration of higher reported death counts in South and East Asia, while vast regions of the Northern Hemisphere and Oceania report zero cases. Reported deaths are limited by surveillance capacity.
